author: @WillardWallace-y5g
text: Jeez, after all the right hand rule stuff, would've thought u got the spin direction correct. The missing shroud should have a flared exit vent at the bottom rear. The air deflectors on the front r at the incorrect pitch & shape. The bottom 1 still needs 2b mounted 2 the bottom of the sill, but it needs 2 b shaped like a race car wing, with the fast side facing up, aimed at the top half of the wheel above the axle. Mount a second wind w the fast side facing down on the top of the shroud. Both wings 2gether not only increase the wind speed via the aerodynamic principle, but also compress it as its forced into a laminar stream, increasing the pressure inside the shroud housing allowing it 2 spin faster. This horizontal design generates more power the faster it spins, & can't suffer from ovr speed failures.

text: You can calculate the energy stored in wind by 1/2*mass*v^2.  mass in kg and v is velocity in m/sec.  a cubic meter of air at sea level is 1.225 kg/m^3. A 30 mi/hr wind has about 110 watts per m^2 if you bring the wind to a stop and extract all of the energy.  Let's assume we want a one kw from a 30 mi/hr wind.  Let's assume we can extract half of the energy.  That means you will need 18 square meters for your wind turbine.  That's 4.3 m per side of a square wind turbine.

author: @trueriver1950
text:
I disagree with your suggestion to deploy it with a horizontal axis.

If you deploy that with the axis horizontal, it will not face the wind from all possible directions. To adjust that you would need to add a so called "yaw" mechanism to turn the drum to face into the wind. On a much lighter windmill this could be a simple weather vane at the back: but however you turn it you also need a vertical yaw axis for the whole device to swivel around. So any horizontal axis turbine ends up having two rotation axes, the fast one is horizontal but a slower vertical yaw axis as well.

In contrast, with the axis vertical you do not need the yaw mechanism at all -- as the wind blows horizontally, the turbine always faces the wind wherever it comes from at that moment. This is why DIY turbines are much more often designed to have a vertical turbine axis.

author: @trueriver1950
text:
power is proportional to windspeed CUBED, so if the wind goes from 10 to 20 which is x2 then you expect the power to go x8, for example. It is that cube factor that makes low windspeeds so disappointing.

For the physicists and engineers, the cube factor comes as follows. Kinetic energy of a given mass of air goes as the square of the speed so is proportional to v squared. BUT THEN the mass of air passing is proportional to the windspeed, bumping the square up to a cube. 

All that assumes that, however (in)efficient your turbine is, it retains roughly the same efficiency across all windspeeds.

author: @scottcary2157
text:
I like your wind turbine, however I don't think you will achieve a 1kw turbine with that size. I would recommend doubling the height of that turbine to achieve your desired output.

This style of turbine will be able to handle gust better but rotor efficiency may be on the lower side without any lift effects.

Math and Engineering part:

Wind turbines are rated at 10.5m/s(~23.8mph) to 16m/s(~35.8mph). 
The formula for the power in the wind is 0.5*(wind density)*(wind area)*(wind speed)^3.
For this type of turbine the wind area is ((turbine width)*(turbine height)).
This works out to be 0.5*(1.2kg/m^3)*(1m*1m)*(16m/s)^3=2.47kw. 

Now to take efficiencies into account:
The maximum efficiency of a rotor can not exceed 59.26% this is called the Betz Limit.
2.47kw*59.26%=1.47kw (Theoretical limit).
Typical efficiency of a rotor are 25%-45%.
2.47kw*25%=0.618kw
2.47kw*45%=1.11kw
generator efficiency ?-95%.
assuming typical 80% efficiency.
0.618kw*80%=0.49kw
1.11kw*80%=0.89kw

author: @Roel922

To choose between a horizontal axis or vertical axis turbine depends on the type of alternator you put un this. I'm working on a axial flux generator and these are most suitable for the type of turbine demonstrated in this video. A radial flux generator is more suitabe to attach to traditional windturbine. I have a small 10Wp radial flux generator already build to learn something about electrical engineering not for any real purposes. I think a horizontal axis turbinedemonstrated in this video has so much advantages over a traditional one. 
1. you don't have any tension on the rot that holds the rotor of the alternator in place while tradition turbine does due to the gravitational pull on the turbine which lowers the power output.
2. they are less likely to cause complains from nabours.
3. they are less dangerous because the turbine plates are hold together by two sides. 
4. It does'n matter where the wind is coming from or is turbulent so you could place them everywhere you want such as on buildings.

What I find also very cool about the way the turbine in this video is made is that you could also use them to generate electricity from rain.

author: @tofo2
text:
To get maximum magnet flux, the magnets should be placed on a common base of iron. The lower the flux the less torque you get, and need more coils for the same torque.

The efficiency is not much affected by magnet arrangement or coils. The coupling between a coil and magnet determines voltage and for each induced voltage you can produce flux proportional to amp*turns. If you replace a full wind coil with half area wire, you can have twice as many turns and twice the voltage, but resistance becomes quadrupled.

It means a thin wire coil has double length wire with half the area and hence 4 times higher resistance. As voltage is doubled with number of turns are doubled it amp*turns is a zero sum game.

Voltage is selected for convenience of what device you connect. Twice the voltage and you can apply or use half the current. Power U*I remains constant.

The voltage of the coil as determined by the wire gauge makes no difference to the performance of the coil. It is how much flux can be lead through the magnetic core that determines how compact the design is and how much material is needed.

Magnets should be magnetically interconnected at their base, the air gap be kept close to where the magnet material is saturated. If air gap between magnet and solenoid core is made small with strong neodymium magnets, the core may  saturate and part of the motion of the magnet will not induce a variating flux and hence no voltage and no torque when flux peaks when magnet at center of the core.

North and south poles of magnets should make a closed magnetic loop through the coil magnetic core. Open air magnetic flux swiping across individual coils with no return magnetic field is bad because air is not a great magnetic conductor.

author: @petebarry2207
text: Place a diverter approximately 35-40% across the wind facing side, from the "baffle", toward the center area, between the baffles.  This will offset the wind on the vanes, forcing the wind to go the way it needs to go, to get the optimum speed.

author: @oddjobbobb
text:
I am really really enjoying this series Robert! It is vintage Dread Mad Scientist Robert. And I can’t help commenting after your insights at about 2:30 in. Like most of my comments this is a little far a field. If you read on, consider yourself warned. 

Similar to how the wind generator’s marketeer’s “theoretical” will vary greatly with the user’s “reality” the same happens between the marketeers promises and users experience of tankless “on-demand” hot water heaters. 

A new Rinnai claims to produce 10 gallons-a-minute (38 liters). But here are the conditions:
1. The incoming water temperature must be 57 degrees F (14 C). This occurs in the United States at about the latitude at the top of Tennessee (and likely nowhere in Great Britain). (That’s the average, so a few degrees warmer in the summer and several degrees colder in winter.) 
2. More important though is the temperature difference between incoming and outlet at the faucet or shower or dishwasher. If you have 57 degree F incoming water and you want 115 (46 C) degree water at your shower (absolute minimum I would say) in the morning, your flow rate is now at 6 gallons a minute (22 l). That’s not awful because the average shower head in the United States uses a bit more than 2 gallons a minute (7.5-ish l).
In Seattle, Washington, where I live, in January the incoming city water supply is around 45-50 degrees F (7.2-10 F) and my minimum temperature for a shower is 120 F (49 C). Now the flow rate is 5 gallons a minute. 
3. Those numbers are for a top of the line, state of the art Rinnai RU199in, $1666 on Amazon and you will pay that much at least for installation. 
4. Finally, unless your natural gas line and meter was installed with a tankless water heater in mind you will likely need a bigger line from the city main and a bigger meter.

The sales person won’t inform the buyer of the real world numbers. The salesperson likely only knows the ideal theoretical numbers. And for sure NO ONE at Amazon will know any of them. 
This is why, you see so many disgruntled angry reviews for tankless hot water heaters EVERYWHERE. The customer over expected the flow rate. The customer under estimated the temperature differential (or more likely didn’t even know). The tankless unit itself is under fueled so the flame drops out then restarts then drops out. And what do you suppose is happening at the shower?
This is not meant as a rant. My tankless runs flawlessly, producing 135 degree water in the winter sufficient to have a shower, the dishwasher and the washing machine all going at the same time. 

My point is, whatever the wind generator salesperson promises, divide by two (to allow for the marketeers lies, misinformation, and ignorance), then reduce your expectations by 10% (to be safe 20% is better). Then look for the wind generator that will serve your needs.
Or better yet, follow along with The Dread Mad Scientist Robert and make your own suited to your needs.
Thank you again Robert for sharing your joy and your brain. Your YTs always delight and never fail to inspire.

author: @flickpad
text: I built something like this on a smaller scale about twenty years ago.  I'll be interested to see your approach with the stator.  Lots of home-brew wind turbine info suggests winding multiple coils and overlaying them. I found this messy and complicated.  I finished up laying two rows of pins around the perimeter which were spaced at the same pitch as the magnets and then ran a continuous conductor out towards the edge and then back towards the middle on the next pair of pins and continued around the stator several times.  I stuck it all down with epoxy and then removed the pins.  (This would be a lot easier to explain with a diagram)  That provided one phase and worked really well.  It might be more challenging to attempt this with the gauge of wire that you'll be using.  I did begin a three phase version where I had the same configuration repeated three times at 1/3 the pitch of the magnets, but then I got a proper job and the project ran out of steam.  I've still got a few bits laying around.

Set your desired output by adjusting spring tension or adding/removing mass etc. negative feedback engineering 101.

text: Your wheel is out of balance.  It is heavy enough on 1 side to cause it to settle when it is on its side.  That could cause vibrations that would cause inefficiencies and, at the extreme end, shake itself apart.  Let the heavy part of the wheel stop at low side and add weights to the high side.  Rinse and repeat until it doesn't want to settle to one side.

author: @kenrowe167
text: Hi Rob. Pretty impressive. And the build standard looks really good. One thing, though :- the baffle plates are normally oriented so as to direct the airstream into the rotor on the downwind (drive) side and away from the rotor on the upwind (drag) side so as to minimise the adverse drag. The way you have them oriented on the upwind side will actually increase the adverse drag. I hope you don't think I'm nitpicking, I'm just trying to be helpful. I'm sure it;s going to work well.

author: @ThomasAndersonbsf
text: now if only you had larger blades and those 4 struts were running across so they reached half way to the middle of your windmill's axial center, from the side that the back of your blades (the ones the wind hits and slows it down, just not as much as the other due to the positive curve vs the other sides negative curve) then you would be getting real "increase size to get more power or speed output" because otherwise the bigger you go, the more resistance to spin you get from the wind hitting your drum on the "wrong side" of the drum, which means this design offers a chance to even use that benefit as I was talking about, :) (angled so they look like a wind mill from overtop instead of an X that way the wind buffets off of them and into the blades of the turbine on their wind catching side and away from the wind slipping side maybe) anyway that was what  I was talking about doing, though I was also looking at allowing that to rotate a bit too so it could wind vein around so the V shape they form to funnel the wind to that propulsion side of the turbine blades, was able to rotate so it forms the top of its V shape pointed directed into the wind, but in your design they could still be the walls to keep the top ridged that holds onto your top bearing too, and not require anything else to keep it from wobbling, (the outer blades are not going to move anywhere near as fast so I was just planning on either a lazy susan bearing, or a bunch of roller bearings turned sideways with a shaft so it allowed the bottom to roll around on them like the long conveyer belt things they use to unload trucks with boxes by dropping them on it and rails on the sides keep the boxes on the path but the bottom of the box is sliding across wheels that keep it moving down hill till it gets into the store or warehouse, just that mine would allow a continuous rotation in a circle lol

author: @jonh2944
text: A super simple built so far, i can't wait to find out what it can generate. When it comes to the electrical side i have limited knowledge...How will the approx 30 voltage with the 60ma...charge a 12v battery? I'm assuming with high RPM the amp will increase alot? Fantastic build & keep up the good work, can't wait until the next installment 👍

replies.author: @hempev
replies.text:
30V would have to go through a dc:dc converter, and since 12V is charged at higher voltage (14.4 to 15V, depending on flooded vs sealed lead-acid), you would set to that output voltage; 60ma gives 1.8W (less conversion inefficiencies), a safe wattage to trickle charge. 

Lithium and nickel chemistries need totally different charging profiles and fancier regulation.

replies.author: @JSabh
replies.text: Yes but, then the cogging from the magnets would increase. So, yes more power output but, it would require much higher starting wind speed and due to that limiting factor, would not be as efficient. Also would be useless in winds under 15 MPH. If you consider that the lost power generation with lighter winds could have at least made 100 to 150 watts an hour on a low wind day, then it is obvious that the need for higher wind speeds is a big set back to the goal. Instead, either make the base wider/ turbine taller ( more surface area ) or install multiple given how cheap they can be made so that you get power out of them in low winds as well.

author: @michaelpearson5465
text:
Power from the wind can be calculated using the equation 

Power = 0.5 × air density (average 1.3 kg m^-3) × area through which the wind is passing × the speed (m s^-1) of the air ^3

Hope this is somewhat helpful, I think this build is brilliant thanks for sharing.

replies.author: @IrishSkruffles
replies.text: I think the velocity of the air should be to the power of 3 but yes that is along the right lines

replies.author: @michaelpearson5465
replies.text: @IrishSkruffles typo on my part well spotted thanks for pointing this out. Although as we aren't specifying a direction of travel it is speed not velocity although its commonly misused, much in the way we say the weight of something when in fact we mean mass.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: very helpful mate - thanks for doing that

replies.author: @michaelpearson5465
replies.text:
Desmond Bagley quite possibly yes but the area that is swept could be calculated by re arranging the formula to make area the subject, once an average energy output is measured. This would then give you the information you need to calculate power at different wind speeds for this particular device. My gut feeling is that the area of the disc will give a fairly good estimate of power available.

Its also worth baring in mind that this is simply a mathematical model for calculating available energy and therfore will not give an exact measurement of power output as we are making assumptions about the conditions, for example the density of the air, neither does it allow for the efficency of the turbine. What this does is give a good idea of what to expect from the turbine. Only further testing would give an more accurate picture of what it truly is capable of.

author: @JSabh
text: Nice, the issue I see you running into is the pressure envelope that will limit the RPM regardless of wind speed with a VAWT, obviously the reason the  a HORIZONTAL axis is used is the ability to overspeed. That is why one does not see VAWT in large commercial use often. I personally love the VAWT due to the self stabilization at high speeds. The blowing up thing is not ideal with over speed of HAWT. You will get around 200 watts hopefully ( at full potential and optimal wind, AKA not 1KW ). We shall see. Also, one could simply attach a belt or chain to the outer rim where you placed the magnets, then to a motor and voila, you have an interchangeable gen-set with simple maintenance. Of course, you would also need to strengthen the turbine axle. Anyway, cheers.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
